byline--multiple-avatars"><div class="avatars-container"><a class="avatar" tabindex="-1" aria-hidden="true" href="/author/ahmed_el_amraoui_201111253277998205"><div class="responsive-image"><img href="/author/faisal_edroos_2014113133857220245"><div class="responsive-image"><img loading="lazy" src="/wp-content/uploads/2016/08/815461339fc0403abb79f647bd6fbfa8_6.jpeg?resize=96%2C96&quality=80" srcSet="/wp-content/uploads/2016/08/815461339fc0403abb79f647bd6fbfa8_6.jpeg?resize=96%2C96&quality=80 96w" sizes="(max-width: 96px) 96px, 96px" aria-hidden="true"/></div></a></div><div class="byline-content"><div class="contributors-list contributors-list--byline"><span class="contributors-list__by-prefix">By </span><a href="/author/ahmed_el_amraoui_201111253277998205">Ahmed El Amraoui</a> and <a href="/author/faisal_edroos_2014113133857220245">Faisal Edroos</a></div></div></div><div class="article-dates"><div class="date-simple"><span class="screen-reader-text">Published On 3 Jun 2018</span><span aria-hidden="true">3 Jun 2018</span></div></div></div><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><p><strong>Istanbul, Turkey –</strong> When <a href="/programmes/general/2007/07/2008525183433170666.html">Mustafa Kemal Ataturk</a> founded the Turkish Republic nearly a century ago, the former military leader banished religion from the public sphere and looked westwards to Europe for inspiration.</p><p>After replacing Islamic law (sharia) with European civil codes, he installed the principles of secularism into the Constitution, banned the Muslim call to prayer in Arabic and pushed for the social integration of the sexes, reforms which would radically alter the fabric of the Muslim-majority country which only years earlier was the seat of the Ottoman Empire.</p><div class="recommended recommended__loading"><div class="loading-cards" data-testid="loading-cards"><div class="loading-card keep-reading"><p class="horizontal-line excerpt-top-line"></p><p class="horizontal-line excerpt-mid-line"></p><p class="horizontal-line excerpt-mid-line"></p><p class="horizontal-line excerpt-bottom-line"></p></div></div></div><p>And now, as <a href="/topics/country/turkey.html">Turkey</a> prepares for crucial presidential and parliamentary elections, the debate as to whether the Muslim world’s model democracy is abandoning its secular model has resurfaced.</p><p>For more than 16 years, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan and his AK Party have dominated the country’s politics, rendering the constitutionally secular country more religious and conservative.</p><div class="inpage-embed article-guid" data-guid="2015122105719549120">
<div style="display:none">{articleGUID}</div>
</div><p>But with millions of Turks expected to head to the polls on June 24, that successful juggling-act is being questioned.</p><p>Opposition groups have begun depicting the 64-year-old President as a ‘Caliph-in-waiting’ who seeks sweeping powers to change the country’s laws to greater reflect its growing Muslim identity.</p><p>“There is a major paradox in the AK Party leadership,” says Baris Yarkadas, an MP with the left-leaning and secularist CHP (Republican People’s Party).</p><p>“One the one hand, the AK Party claims to be secular. On the other, they disagree with several of Mustafa Kemal Ataturk’s views and want to tear down the republic.”</p><blockquote>
<p>
				”Since			</p>
<p class="quote-author">
				<span class="screen-reader-text">by</span> ”Fatma			</p>
</blockquote><p>		 Muslims can enjoy the same rights as others Turkish citizens”]</p><div class="container--ads in-article-ads"><div class="ads"><span class="ads__title"> Advertisement </span></div></div><h2>Overthrowing Ataturk’s legacy?</h2><p>Erdogan has repeatedly rebuked the claims, but a victory for his governing AK Party could reshape Turkish democracy for years to come.</p><p>Such a prospect is an anathema to secularists, with many asking whether Turkey is erasing the line between religion and state.</p><p>“Should they usher in an executive presidency they could overthrow the legacy of Ataturk,” Yarkadas said.</p><p>Unencumbered by religion, Ataturk promoted ancient Turkish traditions and cultures as part of a series of reforms with the intent that nationalism would overcome the idea of an Islamic Ummah, or global Muslim community.</p><div data-embed-type="Brightcove" data-embed-id="5044124906001">
<table class="in-article-item video" border="0"><tbody><tr><td>
		<p></p>
<div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:100%">
<div class="video-player-facade-container"></div>
<p></p></div>
<p>						</p>
</td></tr><tr><td>
<div class="meta"><strong>TALK TO AL JAZEERA:</strong> <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Lq3NOJ3LOPA" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Turkish democracy is not under threat (35:40)</a></div>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
</div><p> Building a state around a cadre of secularised elites, his ideas – known as Kemalism – <span>blamed the demise of the Ottoman Empire on religion and </span>altered virtually every aspect of Turkish life for the next eight decades.</p><p>Under several regimes, the political and legal system he founded prevented Muslims from exerting any significant influence in temporal affairs, with laws and the threat of force silencing pro-Islamic politicians.</p><p>“Secularism was forced on the people as if it were a religion,” says Fatma Benli, an Istanbul-based MP with the AK Party.</p><p>Ataturk outlawed men from wearing the fez, and famously adopted a Panama hat to accent his Western suit and tie; a sample of the many ideas Benli says, which marginalised the predominantly Muslim population.</p><p>“I couldn’t go [to] the garden of my school because I wore the hijab,” she says, describing a period during [the] 1980s and 1990s when the headscarf was banned in government offices, hospitals, universities, and schools.</p><p>“Since the AK Party came to power the situation has normalised, and [practicing] Muslims can enjoy the same rights as other Turkish citizens,” she told Al Jazeera.</p><p>The AK Party, which styles itself as the ideological equivalent of a western European conservative Christian Democrat party, was formed in 2001, after its predecessor – the Virtue Party was shut down by the country’s constitutional court due to its “anti-secular” activities.</p><p>It would steamroll to victory a year later, and push through liberal reforms, achieve unprecedented economic success – transforming a crisis-hit economy into one fuelled by trade and foreign investment – and sideline a military that had undermined Turkish democracy in a series of coups <span>between 1960, 1971, 1980 and a ‘post-modern coup’ in 1997.</span></p><h2>‘Still inherently secular’</h2><p>Over the 16 years that it has since been in power, the AK Party has been mostly pragmatic on the issue of religion and insists the country will remain secular, even if the country switches to an executive presidency.</p><div class="container--ads in-article-ads"><div class="ads"><span class="ads__title"> Advertisement </span></div></div><p>I<span>n 2016, when AK Party politician and parliament speaker Ismail Kahraman suggested that the “irrevocable principles” enshrining secularism be removed from the constitution, Erdogan distanced himself from the remarks.</span></p><div class="inpage-embed article-guid" data-guid="170114085009105">
<div style="display:none">{articleGUID}</div>
</div><p>“Turkey may appear more Islamic now than it was a few decades ago, but when we look at the fabric of Turkish society, the country’s politics are still inherently secular,” says liberal Islamic theologian Ihsan Eliacik.</p><p>“In the last referendum, the ruling party won by only a small majority. And this illustrates how divided Turkish society is.</p><p>“Many people don’t ascribe to Islamic values and in the upcoming election, there is a real possibility the AK party could lose its majority in parliament. Should this happen it will clearly defeat this notion that Turkey is an Islamist State.”</p><p>Benli, the AK Party MP, adds that it is reductive and outdated to speak of Turkish politics strictly in terms of a divide between religion and secularism.</p><p>“Turkey has always been divided into groups, whether they be Turkish, Kurd, secular or non-secular. Such labels are incredibly dangerous and divisive.</p><p>“The AK party always has and continues to maintain that it is a democratic party.”</p><p>Erdogan’s supporters, however, argue an executive-presidency could enable the AK Party to revisit several controversial issues, and promote the socially conservative values and sentiments many hold.</p><p>‘”Turkey is not Iran,” says Mehmet Can, a 30-year-old computer programmer and AK Party supporter.</p><p>“Turkey practices its own brand of secularism and is simply challenging militant secularism.”</p><h2>Protecting ‘pious’ citizens</h2><p>A common theme in Erdogan’s recent election speeches and rallies has been the dark period between the 1920s and early 2000s, when the secular elite ruled the country while so-called “pious” citizens were marginalised.</p><p>The military long considered secularism as a tool for the survival of the elite, and acted as the main deterrent should the outward trappings of conservatism rear its head.</p><div data-embed-type="Brightcove" data-embed-id="1745958204001">
<table class="in-article-item video" border="0"><tbody><tr><td>
		<p></p>
<div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:100%">
<div class="video-player-facade-container"></div>
<p></p></div>
<p>						</p>
</td></tr><tr><td>
<div class="meta">Can Turkey strike a balance between the country’s modern, secular aspirations and its deep-rooted Islamic identity?</div>
</td></tr></tbody></table>
</div><p>After decades of brutal repression, the early 1990s witnessed a return of Islamic practices in the public sphere, but various power struggles remain centred on the issue since then.</p><p> Waiting in the wings, the military toppled elected governments three times (<span>and a ‘post-modern coup’ in 1997)</span>, claiming responsibility as secularism’s ultimate guarantor.</p><p>When the country’s first pro-Islamic prime minister, Necmettin Erbakan, was deemed to have veered too far from the secular path in 1997, generals forced him from power.</p><p>“The idea that secularism is now under threat is highly questionable,” says Sevinc Dogan, an academic and author of the book, titled AKP in the neighbourhood.</p><p>The president has repeatedly said he is committed to secularism, but does not think it should be at the expense of Turks who want to express their religious beliefs in a more open manner.</p><div class="container--connatix-ads"><div class="container--ads in-article-ads slot-3-placement"><div class="ads"><span class="ads__title"> Advertisement </span></div></div></div><p>“The CHP party (the party of Mustafa Kemal) has formed a coalition with the Saadet Party (Felicity Party) – a group which is far more conservative than the governing AK Party.</p><p>“So if the CHP – the party of Mustafa Kemal – fears the threat posed to secularism by Islamist groups, why would they be in an alliance with an Islamist group?” Dogan says.</p><div data-embed-type="Brightcove" data-embed-id="5169904163001">

		<p></p>
<div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:100%">

<p></p></div>

</td></tr><tr><td>

</td></tr></tbody></table>

gold_standard.gold_text:
As elections near, the president has been nicknamed a ‘Caliph in waiting,’ reigniting the debate over Turkey’s identity. Istanbul, Turkey – When Mustafa Kemal Ataturk founded the Turkish Republic nearly a century ago, the former military leader banished religion from the public sphere and looked westwards to Europe for inspiration.

After replacing Islamic law (sharia) with European civil codes, he installed the principles of secularism into the Constitution, banned the Muslim call to prayer in Arabic and pushed for the social integration of the sexes, reforms which would radically alter the fabric of the Muslim-majority country which only years earlier was the seat of the Ottoman Empire.And now, as Turkey prepares for crucial presidential and parliamentary elections, the debate as to whether the Muslim world’s model democracy is abandoning its secular model has resurfaced.

For more than 16 years, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan and his AK Party have dominated the country’s politics, rendering the constitutionally secular country more religious and conservative.

But with millions of Turks expected to head to the polls on June 24, that successful juggling-act is being questioned.

Opposition groups have begun depicting the 64-year-old President as a ‘Caliph-in-waiting’ who seeks sweeping powers to change the country’s laws to greater reflect its growing Muslim identity.

“There is a major paradox in the AK Party leadership,” says Baris Yarkadas, an MP with the left-leaning and secularist CHP (Republican People’s Party).

“One the one hand, the AK Party claims to be secular. On the other, they disagree with several of Mustafa Kemal Ataturk’s views and want to tear down the republic.” Muslims can enjoy the same rights as others Turkish citizens”] Overthrowing Ataturk’s legacy?

Erdogan has repeatedly rebuked the claims, but a victory for his governing AK Party could reshape Turkish democracy for years to come. Such a prospect is an anathema to secularists, with many asking whether Turkey is erasing the line between religion and state.

“Should they usher in an executive presidency they could overthrow the legacy of Ataturk,” Yarkadas said.

Unencumbered by religion, Ataturk promoted ancient Turkish traditions and cultures as part of a series of reforms with the intent that nationalism would overcome the idea of an Islamic Ummah, or global Muslim community. TALK TO AL JAZEERA: Turkish democracy is not under threat (35:40)

Building a state around a cadre of secularised elites, his ideas – known as Kemalism – blamed the demise of the Ottoman Empire on religion and altered virtually every aspect of Turkish life for the next eight decades.

Under several regimes, the political and legal system he founded prevented Muslims from exerting any significant influence in temporal affairs, with laws and the threat of force silencing pro-Islamic politicians.

“Secularism was forced on the people as if it were a religion,” says Fatma Benli, an Istanbul-based MP with the AK Party.

Ataturk outlawed men from wearing the fez, and famously adopted a Panama hat to accent his Western suit and tie; a sample of the many ideas Benli says, which marginalised the predominantly Muslim population.

“I couldn’t go [to] the garden of my school because I wore the hijab,” she says, describing a period during [the] 1980s and 1990s when the headscarf was banned in government offices, hospitals, universities, and schools.

“Since the AK Party came to power the situation has normalised, and [practicing] Muslims can enjoy the same rights as other Turkish citizens,” she told Al Jazeera.

The AK Party, which styles itself as the ideological equivalent of a western European conservative Christian Democrat party, was formed in 2001, after its predecessor – the Virtue Party was shut down by the country’s constitutional court due to its “anti-secular” activities.

It would steamroll to victory a year later, and push through liberal reforms, achieve unprecedented economic success – transforming a crisis-hit economy into one fuelled by trade and foreign investment – and sideline a military that had undermined Turkish democracy in a series of coups between 1960, 1971, 1980 and a ‘post-modern coup’ in 1997.
‘Still inherently secular’

Over the 16 years that it has since been in power, the AK Party has been mostly pragmatic on the issue of religion and insists the country will remain secular, even if the country switches to an executive presidency. In 2016, when AK Party politician and parliament speaker Ismail Kahraman suggested that the “irrevocable principles” enshrining secularism be removed from the constitution, Erdogan distanced himself from the remarks.

“Turkey may appear more Islamic now than it was a few decades ago, but when we look at the fabric of Turkish society, the country’s politics are still inherently secular,” says liberal Islamic theologian Ihsan Eliacik.

“In the last referendum, the ruling party won by only a small majority. And this illustrates how divided Turkish society is.

“Many people don’t ascribe to Islamic values and in the upcoming election, there is a real possibility the AK party could lose its majority in parliament. Should this happen it will clearly defeat this notion that Turkey is an Islamist State.”

Benli, the AK Party MP, adds that it is reductive and outdated to speak of Turkish politics strictly in terms of a divide between religion and secularism.

“Turkey has always been divided into groups, whether they be Turkish, Kurd, secular or non-secular. Such labels are incredibly dangerous and divisive.

“The AK party always has and continues to maintain that it is a democratic party.”

Erdogan’s supporters, however, argue an executive-presidency could enable the AK Party to revisit several controversial issues, and promote the socially conservative values and sentiments many hold.

‘”Turkey is not Iran,” says Mehmet Can, a 30-year-old computer programmer and AK Party supporter.

“Turkey practices its own brand of secularism and is simply challenging militant secularism.”
Protecting ‘pious’ citizens

A common theme in Erdogan’s recent election speeches and rallies has been the dark period between the 1920s and early 2000s, when the secular elite ruled the country while so-called “pious” citizens were marginalised.

The military long considered secularism as a tool for the survival of the elite, and acted as the main deterrent should the outward trappings of conservatism rear its head. Can Turkey strike a balance between the country’s modern, secular aspirations and its deep-rooted Islamic identity?

After decades of brutal repression, the early 1990s witnessed a return of Islamic practices in the public sphere, but various power struggles remain centred on the issue since then.

Waiting in the wings, the military toppled elected governments three times (and a ‘post-modern coup’ in 1997), claiming responsibility as secularism’s ultimate guarantor.

When the country’s first pro-Islamic prime minister, Necmettin Erbakan, was deemed to have veered too far from the secular path in 1997, generals forced him from power.

“The idea that secularism is now under threat is highly questionable,” says Sevinc Dogan, an academic and author of the book, titled AKP in the neighbourhood.

The president has repeatedly said he is committed to secularism, but does not think it should be at the expense of Turks who want to express their religious beliefs in a more open manner. “The CHP party (the party of Mustafa Kemal) has formed a coalition with the Saadet Party (Felicity Party) – a group which is far more conservative than the governing AK Party.

“So if the CHP – the party of Mustafa Kemal – fears the threat posed to secularism by Islamist groups, why would they be in an alliance with an Islamist group?” Dogan says.

m--b-mobile-20"><div class="byline byline--single-avatar"><div class="avatars-container"><a class="avatar" tabindex="-1" aria-hidden="true" href="/author/al_jazeera_staff_150119130629458"><div class="responsive-image"><img loading="lazy" src="/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/2017-06-08T131939Z_1189275883_RC11B0FE7F20_RTRMADP_3_GULF-QATAR-JAZEERA-1714943623.jpg?resize=96%2C96&quality=80" srcSet="/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/2017-06-08T131939Z_1189275883_RC11B0FE7F20_RTRMADP_3_GULF-QATAR-JAZEERA-1714943623.jpg?resize=96%2C96&quality=80 96w" sizes="(max-width: 96px) 96px, 96px" aria-hidden="true"/></div></a></div><div class="byline-content"><div class="contributors-list contributors-list--byline"><span class="contributors-list__by-prefix">By </span><a href="/author/al_jazeera_staff_150119130629458">Al Jazeera Staff</a></div></div></div><div class="article-dates"><div class="date-simple"><span class="screen-reader-text">Published On 17 Apr 2026</span><span aria-hidden="true">17 Apr 2026</span></div></div></div><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><p><strong>Washington, DC –</strong> United States President Donald Trump has posted a series of rapid-fire messages on the US-Israeli war with Iran, claiming that his government secured major concessions before a <a href="/news/2026/4/16/no-date-set-for-us-iran-talks-as-pakistan-pushes-to-keep-diplomacy-alive">possible next round</a> of ceasefire talks.</p><p>Posted on Truth Social on Friday, Trump’s statements <a href="https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116420562510387829" target="_blank" rel="noopener">claimed</a> that Iran had agreed to open — and “never close” — the Strait of Hormuz.</p><section class="more-on"><h2 class="more-on__heading">Recommended Stories<!-- --> </h2><span class="screen-reader-text">list of 3 items</span><ul class="more-on__list"><li class="more-on__article"><span class="screen-reader-text">list 1 of 3</span><a class="more-on__link" href="/opinions/2026/4/17/the-iran-war-has-exposed-the-limits-of-neutrality">The Iran war has exposed the limits of neutrality</a></li><li class="more-on__article"><span class="screen-reader-text">list 2 of 3</span><a class="more-on__link" href="/news/2026/4/17/macron-and-starmer-host-allies-for-summit-on-hormuz-maritime-security">Macron and Starmer host allies for summit on Hormuz maritime security</a></li><li class="more-on__article"><span class="screen-reader-text">list 3 of 3</span><a class="more-on__link" href="/opinions/2026/4/17/the-three-clocks-of-the-iran-war">The three clocks of the Iran war</a></li></ul><span class="screen-reader-text">end of list</span></section><p>He also <a href="https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116420395293904982" target="_blank" rel="noopener">alleged</a> that Tehran would turn over its “nuclear dust” and that Israel would be “prohibited” from launching attacks in Lebanon.</p><p>Iran has confirmed reopening the Strait of Hormuz for the “duration” of the current pause in fighting, which is set to end early next week, barring a new agreement. However, officials have pushed back on claims regarding its nuclear stockpile, with a source telling Al Jazeera negotiations remain at a preliminary stage.</p><p>Despite the outstanding questions, the US president struck a celebratory tone, calling Friday “A GREAT AND BRILLIANT DAY FOR THE WORLD!”</p><p>Separately, he told Bloomberg News that he expected talks to move forward on Sunday with a permanent ceasefire deal in sight.</p><p>“We’re not seeing the full picture,” Yezid Sayigh, a senior fellow at the Carnegie Middle East Center, told Al Jazeera, pointing to Trump’s penchant for hyperbole and several unresolved issues.</p><p>“But this does suggest a positive momentum towards something that may end up being a comprehensive deal.”</p><p>		</p><div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:770px">
<div class="video-player-facade-container"></div>
<p></p></div><p>						</p><p>Sayigh added that Trump could have ulterior motives in striking an upbeat tone, at a time when the chokehold in the Strait of Hormuz is driving up prices for everything from fuel to fertiliser.</p><div class="container--ads in-article-ads"><div class="ads"><span class="ads__title"> Advertisement </span></div></div><p>“It is very interesting that President Trump is putting such a positive spin on things, not only to encourage markets and talk down oil prices and talk stock market prices up,” Sayigh said.</p><p>“But also, I suspect, because he’s preparing the ground for more revelations about what is being negotiated with Iran.”</p><h2 id="trump-says-iran-will-never-close-strait-of-hormuz">Trump says Iran will ‘never close’ Strait of Hormuz</h2><p>Part of Trump’s social media claims appeared to be confirmed in a separate statement from Tehran. But the details showed distance between Trump’s position and Iran’s.</p><p>Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi announced on Friday that, in light of a <a href="/news/2026/4/17/what-we-know-about-the-israel-lebanon-ceasefire">10-day pause</a> in fighting in Lebanon, “the passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz is declared completely open for the remaining period of ceasefire.”</p><p>Trump swiftly echoed that claim on social media, writing that the strait is “FULLY OPEN AND READY FOR FULL PASSAGE”.</p><p>However, a senior Iranian military official told state media that only nonmilitary vessels would be allowed to transit the strait — and only with permission from the Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) Navy.</p><p>Then, Trump went further with his claims of free maritime traffic, saying, “Iran has agreed to never close the Strait of Hormuz again.”</p><p>		</p><div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:770px">
<div class="video-player-facade-container"></div>
<p></p></div><p>						</p><p>Speaking to Al Jazeera, Alexandru Hudisteanu, a maritime security expert, said there has been no confirmation from Iran that such a long-term pledge had been made.</p><p>“We know, for instance, that over the last six weeks or so of the war, the Iranian leadership grasped the fact that its control over the Strait of Hormuz gave it the kind of deterrence that its nuclear programme, its ballistic missiles, its regional proxies, had never actually given it,” he said.</p><p>“So, I don’t think that the Iranian leadership would have made this kind of commitment unequivocally and irrevocably.”</p><p>There were also lingering questions about another post Trump made, concerning the US decision to blockade the strait.</p><p>Trump <a href="https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116420275523158052" target="_blank" rel="noopener">posted</a> that the ongoing US naval blockade of Iranian ports in the Gulf “WILL REMAIN IN FULL FORCE AND EFFECT AS IT PERTAINS TO IRAN, ONLY, UNTIL SUCH TIME AS OUR TRANSACTION WITH IRAN IS 100% COMPLETE”.</p><p>But media reports indicate that Iran might close traffic in the Hormuz Strait if such a blockade were to remain in effect.</p><p>Iran’s Fars News Agency, which is closely aligned with the IRGC, reported that Tehran considers the continuation of the US blockade a violation of the current ceasefire and would again close the strait if it were not lifted.</p><p>Then there were the questions of mines in the strait. On Truth Social, Trump also said that “Iran, with the help of the U.S.A., has removed, or is removing, all sea mines!”</p><div class="container--ads in-article-ads"><div class="ads"><span class="ads__title"> Advertisement </span></div></div><p>But the news agency Reuters reported that a US Navy advisory warned on Friday that the “status of TSS mine threat is not fully understood. Consider avoidance of that area”. The statement referred to the Traffic Separation Scheme, the two-way shipping lane in the strait.</p><h2 id="trump-says-us-will-get-all-nuclear-dust">Trump says US will get all nuclear ‘dust’</h2><p>A major sticking point in the negotiations between the US and Iran has been the future of Iran’s nuclear programme. While Iran has denied seeking a nuclear weapon, the US and Israel have claimed their initial attacks in the war, on February 28, were designed to prevent Iran from constructing one.</p><p>On Friday, Trump again appeared to claim that Iran would turn over its enriched uranium stockpile, writing on Truth Social that “the U.S.A. will get all Nuclear ‘Dust'”.</p><p>The term “dust” appears to be a reference to the US decision to bomb three key nuclear sites in Iran on June 22, 2025. Trump has repeatedly claimed that those attacks “obliterated” Iran’s nuclear programme, despite conflicting evidence.</p><p>On Friday, after his post, Trump told Reuters that the US would work with Iran “at a nice leisurely pace, and go down and start excavating with big machinery” to retrieve the uranium stockpile at the sites.</p><p>“We’ll bring ⁠it back to the United States,” he added. He also told Bloomberg that Iran had agreed to suspend its nuclear programme indefinitely.</p><p>Iran’s Tasnim news agency quoted Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei dismissing Trump’s claim. “Enriched uranium is as sacred to us as Iranian soil and will not be transferred anywhere under any circumstances,” he said.</p><p>Reporting from Tehran, Al Jazeera correspondent Ali Hashem also said there has been no confirmation on such an agreement to transfer Iranian uranium to the US.</p><p>“This is a big claim here, because the Iranians have always said that they’re not going to accept such a condition,” Hashem said. “What we’ve heard from our sources is that the issue of enrichment and the sunsets of any conditions is going to be left till the end of the talks.”</p><p>		</p><div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:770px">
<div class="video-player-facade-container"></div>
<p></p></div><p>						</p><p> </p><p>In the same Truth Social post, Trump also maintained that “no money will exchange hands in any way, shape, or form”, another claim that has not been confirmed by Iran.</p><p>Hashem added that the release of billions of dollars in frozen Iranian funds, the lifting of US sanctions and reparations for war damages have been key priorities for Tehran. He believes Iran will leverage issues like the free flow of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz to extract concessions.</p><p>“What’s the price for this?” Hashem said. “And what are the Iranians going to take in return? These are big questions.”</p><h2 id="trump-says-israel-prohibited-from-bombing-lebanon">Trump says Israel ‘prohibited’ from bombing Lebanon</h2><p>Another issue that has threatened to disrupt the ceasefire talks was Israel’s ongoing bombing campaign in Lebanon. Iran and mediators in Pakistan had maintained that Lebanon was included in the initial ceasefire, but the US and Israel denied that it was part of the agreement.</p><p>But on Thursday, a breakthrough was announced: Israel had agreed to a 10-day pause in its invasion and bombardment of Lebanon.</p><div class="container--connatix-ads"><div class="container--ads in-article-ads slot-3-placement"><div class="ads"><span class="ads__title"> Advertisement </span></div></div></div><p>On Friday, however, Trump seemed to imply that the stoppage was to continue into the long term.</p><p>“Israel will not be bombing Lebanon any longer,” he wrote. “They are PROHIBITED from doing so by the U.S.A. Enough is enough!!!”</p><p>He added that Washington will work with Lebanon and “deal with the Hezboolah situation in an appropriate manner”.</p><p>		</p><div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:770px">
<div class="video-player-facade-container"></div>
<p></p></div><p>						</p><p>Reporting from Washington, DC, Al Jazeera correspondent Alan Fisher said the statement represents a particularly hard US line on Israel, but it remains to be seen if Trump would indeed seek to punish Israel if they do not comply.</p><p>“That’s not what the United States tends to do,” Fisher said.</p><p>“Is Donald Trump actually going to change the norms of American politics when it comes to dealing with the Israelis and treat them like he’s treated other countries in the past, and effectively cut them off?”</p><h2 id="trump-thanks-regional-countries-takes-shot-at-nato">Trump thanks regional countries, takes shot at NATO</h2><p>While Trump’s posts on Friday were largely celebratory, he did hand out darts and laurels to various parties in the ceasefire negotiations.</p><p>Trump thanked Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Qatar for their “great bravery and help”. He also thanked Pakistan’s Prime Minister Shebaz Sharif and Field Marshal Asim Munir, who helped to coordinate US-Iran contacts. He called them “two fantastic people”.</p><p>But Trump took aim at NATO, which he has criticised for not supporting the US and Israeli war against Iran, as well as subsequent efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.</p><p>		</p><div style="display:block;position:relative;min-width:0px;max-width:770px">
<div class="video-player-facade-container"></div>
<p></p></div><p>						</p><p>“Now that the Hormuz Strait situation is over, I received a call from NATO asking if we would need some help. I TOLD THEM TO STAY AWAY, UNLESS THEY JUST WANT TO LOAD UP THEIR SHIPS WITH OIL,” Trump wrote.  “They were useless when needed, a Paper Tiger!”</p><p>The Trump administration has signalled it is mulling a withdrawal from NATO, although such a move would require congressional approval.</p><p>But despite Trump’s most recent statements, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer said European allies had agreed to accelerate military planning for a multinational force to secure the waterway during a summit in Paris on Friday.</p></div></main><hr/><div class="loading-cards" gold_standard.gold_text:
In rapid-fire posts, Trump says Iran will give up nuclear material and ‘never close’ the Hormuz Strait. But questions remain about the accuracy of his claims.
Washington, DC – United States President Donald Trump has posted a series of rapid-fire messages on the US-Israeli war with Iran, claiming that his government secured major concessions before a possible next round of ceasefire talks.
Posted on Truth Social on Friday, Trump’s statements claimed that Iran had agreed to open — and “never close” — the Strait of Hormuz.
He also alleged that Tehran would turn over its “nuclear dust” and that Israel would be “prohibited” from launching attacks in Lebanon.
Iran has confirmed reopening the Strait of Hormuz for the “duration” of the current pause in fighting, which is set to end early next week, barring a new agreement. However, officials have pushed back on claims regarding its nuclear stockpile, with a source telling Al Jazeera negotiations remain at a preliminary stage.
Despite the outstanding questions, the US president struck a celebratory tone, calling Friday “A GREAT AND BRILLIANT DAY FOR THE WORLD!”
Separately, he told Bloomberg News that he expected talks to move forward on Sunday with a permanent ceasefire deal in sight.
“We’re not seeing the full picture,” Yezid Sayigh, a senior fellow at the Carnegie Middle East Center, told Al Jazeera, pointing to Trump’s penchant for hyperbole and several unresolved issues.
“But this does suggest a positive momentum towards something that may end up being a comprehensive deal.”
Sayigh added that Trump could have ulterior motives in striking an upbeat tone, at a time when the chokehold in the Strait of Hormuz is driving up prices for everything from fuel to fertiliser.
“It is very interesting that President Trump is putting such a positive spin on things, not only to encourage markets and talk down oil prices and talk stock market prices up,” Sayigh said.
“But also, I suspect, because he’s preparing the ground for more revelations about what is being negotiated with Iran.”
Trump says Iran will ‘never close’ Strait of Hormuz
Part of Trump’s social media claims appeared to be confirmed in a separate statement from Tehran. But the details showed distance between Trump’s position and Iran’s.
Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi announced on Friday that, in light of a 10-day pause in fighting in Lebanon, “the passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz is declared completely open for the remaining period of ceasefire.”
Trump swiftly echoed that claim on social media, writing that the strait is “FULLY OPEN AND READY FOR FULL PASSAGE”.
However, a senior Iranian military official told state media that only nonmilitary vessels would be allowed to transit the strait — and only with permission from the Iranian Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) Navy.
Then, Trump went further with his claims of free maritime traffic, saying, “Iran has agreed to never close the Strait of Hormuz again.”
Speaking to Al Jazeera, Alexandru Hudisteanu, a maritime security expert, said there has been no confirmation from Iran that such a long-term pledge had been made.
We know, for instance, that over the last six weeks or so of the war, the Iranian leadership grasped the fact that its control over the Strait of Hormuz gave it the kind of deterrence that its nuclear programme, its ballistic missiles, its regional proxies, had never actually given it,” he said.
“So, I don’t think that the Iranian leadership would have made this kind of commitment unequivocally and irrevocably.”
There were also lingering questions about another post Trump made, concerning the US decision to blockade the strait.
Trump posted that the ongoing US naval blockade of Iranian ports in the Gulf “WILL REMAIN IN FULL FORCE AND EFFECT AS IT PERTAINS TO IRAN, ONLY, UNTIL SUCH TIME AS OUR TRANSACTION WITH IRAN IS 100% COMPLETE”.
But media reports indicate that Iran might close traffic in the Hormuz Strait if such a blockade were to remain in effect.
Iran’s Fars News Agency, which is closely aligned with the IRGC, reported that Tehran considers the continuation of the US blockade a violation of the current ceasefire and would again close the strait if it were not lifted.
Then there were the questions of mines in the strait. On Truth Social, Trump also said that “Iran, with the help of the U.S.A., has removed, or is removing, all sea mines!”
But the news agency Reuters reported that a US Navy advisory warned on Friday that the “status of TSS mine threat is not fully understood. Consider avoidance of that area”. The statement referred to the Traffic Separation Scheme, the two-way shipping lane in the strait.
Trump says US will get all nuclear ‘dust’
A major sticking point in the negotiations between the US and Iran has been the future of Iran’s nuclear programme. While Iran has denied seeking a nuclear weapon, the US and Israel have claimed their initial attacks in the war, on February 28, were designed to prevent Iran from constructing one.
On Friday, Trump again appeared to claim that Iran would turn over its enriched uranium stockpile, writing on Truth Social that “the U.S.A. will get all Nuclear ‘Dust'”.
The term “dust” appears to be a reference to the US decision to bomb three key nuclear sites in Iran on June 22, 2025. Trump has repeatedly claimed that those attacks “obliterated” Iran’s nuclear programme, despite conflicting evidence.
On Friday, after his post, Trump told Reuters that the US would work with Iran “at a nice leisurely pace, and go down and start excavating with big machinery” to retrieve the uranium stockpile at the sites.
“We’ll bring ⁠it back to the United States,” he added. He also told Bloomberg that Iran had agreed to suspend its nuclear programme indefinitely.
Iran’s Tasnim news agency quoted Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei dismissing Trump’s claim. “Enriched uranium is as sacred to us as Iranian soil and will not be transferred anywhere under any circumstances,” he said.
Reporting from Tehran, Al Jazeera correspondent Ali Hashem also said there has been no confirmation on such an agreement to transfer Iranian uranium to the US.
“This is a big claim here, because the Iranians have always said that they’re not going to accept such a condition,” Hashem said. “What we’ve heard from our sources is that the issue of enrichment and the sunsets of any conditions is going to be left till the end of the talks.”
In the same Truth Social post, Trump also maintained that “no money will exchange hands in any way, shape, or form”, another claim that has not been confirmed by Iran.
Hashem added that the release of billions of dollars in frozen Iranian funds, the lifting of US sanctions and reparations for war damages have been key priorities for Tehran. He believes Iran will leverage issues like the free flow of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz to extract concessions.
“What’s the price for this?” Hashem said. “And what are the Iranians going to take in return? These are big questions.”
Trump says Israel ‘prohibited’ from bombing Lebanon
Another issue that has threatened to disrupt the ceasefire talks was Israel’s ongoing bombing campaign in Lebanon. Iran and mediators in Pakistan had maintained that Lebanon was included in the initial ceasefire, but the US and Israel denied that it was part of the agreement.
But on Thursday, a breakthrough was announced: Israel had agreed to a 10-day pause in its invasion and bombardment of Lebanon.
On Friday, however, Trump seemed to imply that the stoppage was to continue into the long term.
“Israel will not be bombing Lebanon any longer,” he wrote. “They are PROHIBITED from doing so by the U.S.A. Enough is enough!!!”
He added that Washington will work with Lebanon and “deal with the Hezboolah situation in an appropriate manner”.
Reporting from Washington, DC, Al Jazeera correspondent Alan Fisher said the statement represents a particularly hard US line on Israel, but it remains to be seen if Trump would indeed seek to punish Israel if they do not comply.“That’s not what the United States tends to do,” Fisher said.
“Is Donald Trump actually going to change the norms of American politics when it comes to dealing with the Israelis and treat them like he’s treated other countries in the past, and effectively cut them off?”
Trump thanks regional countries, takes shot at NATO
While Trump’s posts on Friday were largely celebratory, he did hand out darts and laurels to various parties in the ceasefire negotiations.
Trump thanked Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Qatar for their “great bravery and help”. He also thanked Pakistan’s Prime Minister Shebaz Sharif and Field Marshal Asim Munir, who helped to coordinate US-Iran contacts. He called them “two fantastic people”.
But Trump took aim at NATO, which he has criticised for not supporting the US and Israeli war against Iran, as well as subsequent efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
“Now that the Hormuz Strait situation is over, I received a call from NATO asking if we would need some help. I TOLD THEM TO STAY AWAY, UNLESS THEY JUST WANT TO LOAD UP THEIR SHIPS WITH OIL,” Trump wrote.  “They were useless when needed, a Paper Tiger!
The Trump administration has signalled it is mulling a withdrawal from NATO, although such a move would require congressional approval.
But despite Trump’s most recent statements, UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer said European allies had agreed to accelerate military planning for a multinational force to secure the waterway during a summit in Paris on Friday.

class="container__inner"><div class="l-col l-col--8--centered"><div class="longform-text"><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><p>It was a chilly January afternoon in Iowa, and crowds of supporters ringed the stump-speech stage, festooned in red, white and blue.</p><p>The scene could have easily been part of United States President Donald Trump's bid for re-election two years ago.</p><p>But last month, he hit the campaign trail yet again with a different mission: winning the midterm elections for the Republican Party.</p><p>"I'm here because we're starting the campaign to win the midterms," Trump told the crowd. "We've got to win the midterms."</p><p>It is not uncommon for sitting presidents to support their party in the midterm races, even though they themselves are not on the ballot. But as the 2026 midterm primaries approach in March, the Trump administration has made it clear one politician should be centre stage: Trump himself.</p><p>Sources close to the White House have indicated that the president is pursuing an aggressive campaign strategy, one that could include near-weekly public rallies.</p><p>"He's going to campaign like it's 2024 again," Susie Wiles, his chief of staff and former campaign manager, told The Mom View talk show in December.</p><p>Already, Trump visited two critical swing states, Pennsylvania and North Carolina, in December, and he has continued to hold public events in the weeks since, touting his economic record in Iowa and Michigan.</p><p>But the strategy comes with risks. Midterms often bring stinging losses for the sitting president's party, and Democrats are hoping to leverage Trump's wilting poll numbers to chip away at the Republican majorities in both chambers of Congress.</p><p>"The Democratic Party most likely will frame this midterm election as a national referendum on President Trump," said Liberty University law professor Tory L Lucas.</p><p>“The more President Trump plays into that narrative and places himself at the centre of the midterms, the more likely the Republican Party will rise or fall based on his personality and popularity."</p></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="longform-block"><figure class="longform-hero-figure"><div class="longform-hero longform-hero--no-title-summary"><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><h2 id="the-midterm-curse">The midterm curse</h2><p>Part of the rationale behind Trump's plans for heavy campaigning stems from fears of the so-called "midterm curse".</p><p>That refers to the idea that a sitting president's party is doomed to lose seats in Congress during the midterm elections.</p><p>The pattern holds true even with popular presidents, regardless of party. Lucas pointed out that, since World War II, 18 of the 20 midterm elections have caused the sitting president to shed seats in the House of Representatives.</p><p>The two exceptions occurred under Democrat Bill Clinton in 1998 and Republican George W Bush in 2002.</p><p>"The president’s party tends to lose House seats in 90 percent of midterm elections in the modern era," Lucas told Al Jazeera.</p><p>But rather than receding from the spotlight, Lucas indicated that Trump is leaning into his central role in the midterms as a way to buck the trend.</p><p>“By nationalising this race, President Trump is trying to defy the structural gravity that has pulled down predecessors from Franklin D Roosevelt to Barack Obama," he said.</p><p>Trump and his allies have acknowledged their steep odds, gesturing in their remarks to the midterm curse. The president even suggested the slump could be linked to voters' mental health.</p><p>"Sitting presidents don't seem to do well in the midterms. I guess, over a 50-year period, they won twice. So I don't know what that is," Trump told reporters last month on Air Force One.</p><p>"That's something down deep. You'd have to ask really a psychiatrist about that."</p></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="longform-block"><figure class="longform-hero-figure"><div class="longform-hero longform-hero--no-title-summary"><div class="longform-hero__content longform-hero__content--vertically-center longform-hero__content--horizontally-center"><h2 class="longform-hero__title class="longform-text"><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><h2 id="sagging-polls">Sagging polls</h2><p>As the primaries draw closer, though, Trump’s ability to rebound will be put to the test.</p><p>Part of the hurdle will be to recover from his sagging approval ratings, which reflect public backlash on issues that are key to his platform, such as immigration and the economy.</p><p>For instance, a January <a href="https://d3nkl3psvxxpe9.cloudfront.net/documents/econTabReport_8FWGyNz.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener">poll</a> from The Economist and the analytics firm YouGov found that 57 percent of US citizens disapproved of Trump’s job performance. Only 39 percent approved.</p><p>A separate Reuters-Ipsos <a href="https://www.reuters.com/data/trumps-approval-rating-2025-01-21/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">survey</a> from the same month showed similar results, with 59 percent of respondents disapproving of Trump's tenure so far.</p><p>Andra Gillespie, a political science professor at Emory University, believes those statistics should worry Republicans.</p><p>“The signs don’t look favourable this year for Republicans,” she said. “I won’t predict the number of seats, but Republicans should be bracing based on those numbers that they’re going to sustain some losses.”</p><p>Only four seats divide the Republican majority from the Democratic minority in the 435-member House of Representatives.</p><p>In the Senate, Republican control is equally slim. Republicans hold 53 seats, compared to the 47 that caucus with the Democrats.</p><p>The House, however, is particularly vulnerable: All 435 slots are up for grabs in November's midterms, whereas only a third of the Senate is.</p></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="longform-block"><figure class="longform-hero-figure"><div class="longform-hero longform-hero--no-title-summary"><div class="longform-hero__content longform-hero__content--vertically-center longform-hero__content--horizontally-center"><h2 class="longform-hero__title longform-hero__title--large"></h2><p class="longform-text"><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><h2 id="nationalising-the-race">Nationalising the race</h2><p>The Republican Party has suffered losses since Trump's return to the presidency last year.</p><p>In 2025's off-year races, Democrats notched a handful of victories, from Virginia to New Jersey. Wiles, a close adviser to Trump, has blamed the Republican defeats on Trump’s absence from the ballot.</p><p>"Typically, in the midterms, it’s not about who’s sitting in the White House. You localise the election, and you keep federal officials out of it," Wiles explained to The Mom View.</p><p>"We’re actually going to turn that on its head and put him on the ballot, because so many of those low-propensity voters are Trump voters."</p><p>Her strategy is designed to harness the strong sense of loyalty Trump has engendered in the Republican Party.</p><p>The YouGov poll found conservative voters overwhelmingly approved of his job, at a rate of 82 percent. A mid-January CBS News survey found an even higher approval rating — 90 percent — among US adults who identify as Republicans.</p><p>“Since 2016, our surveys have all been off because we underestimate the Trump vote consistently," said political scientist Lonna Rae Atkeson.</p><p>“Trump has definitely drawn more support from irregular voters, people who don't regularly go to the polls, during presidential elections."</p><p>But she questioned whether Trump's endorsement would translate into increased support for down-ballot races.</p><p>"We haven't seen that carry over well to the midterms,” said Atkeson. “So it may not turn out well for him.”</p><p>But putting Trump "on the ballot", as Wiles suggests, also risks shifting the focus of the midterm races away from local issues.</p><p>Instead, experts like Gillespie believe that "nationalising" the midterm races could homogenise both down-ballot candidates and their policy platforms, as they seek to reflect national priorities, not local ones.</p><p>“One manifestation of polarisation in American politics is that national issues increasingly supplant local ones,” Gillespie said. “As national politics seep into state and local races, it becomes harder for federal candidates to distinguish themselves from Washington.”</p></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="longform-block"><figure class="longform-hero-figure"><div class="longform-hero longform-hero--no-title-summary"><div class="longform-hero__content longform-hero__content--vertically-center class="longform-text"><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><h2 id="a-two-way-street">A 'two-way street'</h2><p>Already, Trump has announced dozens of endorsements for Republicans campaigning for the Senate, the House and gubernatorial races.</p><p>As lines become blurred between local and national politics, Republican hopefuls must determine to what degree they will associate with Trump.</p><p>For some candidates in swing districts, proximity to the president could be viewed as a political liability.</p><p>“I think it’s important to keep in mind that this is a two-way street,” said Gillespie.</p><p>"It is going to be up to the candidates themselves to determine the extent to which they align themselves with President Trump."</p><p>Some Republican incumbents, like Representative Mike Lawler of New York, have already put some distance between themselves and the president after Trump posted a racist video on February 5.</p><p>Others, like Republican Thomas Massie of Kentucky, have broken from Trump altogether, leading the president to endorse a primary challenger.</p><p>Gillespie said that, in general, she is sceptical of the efficacy of Trump's backing.</p><p>The majority of his endorsements tend to go to incumbents, who generally have more favourable odds than newcomers.</p><p>"What I’ve observed is, sometimes he has a tendency to endorse people who are already on a trajectory of success, and then he takes credit for it," Gillespie said.</p><p>Atkeson too questioned whether Trump's sweeping plans for midterm rallies will actually help the candidates running.</p><p>"There's not a lot of evidence that his campaigning for people has a big effect. His coattails don’t seem incredibly large," said Atkeson.</p></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="longform-block"><figure class="longform-hero-figure"><div class="longform-hero longform-hero--no-title-summary"><div class="longform-hero__content longform-hero__content--vertically-center longform-hero__content--horizontally-center"><h2 class="longform-hero__title [Kevin Lamarque/Reuters]</div></figcaption></figure></div><div class="longform-block"><div class="container"><div class="container__inner"><div class="l-col l-col--8--centered"><div class="longform-text"><div class="wysiwyg wysiwyg--all-content" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"><h2 id="cooperation-or-confrontation">Cooperation or confrontation?</h2><p>But Trump's motives for aggressive campaigning go beyond simply boosting down-ballot Republicans.</p><p>This year's midterms hold high stakes for the president. Maintaining control of the House could determine whether Trump's legislative proposals move forward — and whether he will face impeachment during the final two years of his term.</p><p>“If voters allow Republicans to keep control of the House, President Trump will have a friendly House,” said Lucas. “But if Democrats take control, increased oversight and impeachment seem like foregone conclusions.”</p><p>Trump himself has warned on multiple occasions that impeachment will be a consequence of losing the midterm races.</p><p>"You've got to win the midterms," Trump told a conference of Republican leaders last month.</p><p>"Because if we don't win the midterms, it's just going to be — I mean, they will find a reason to impeach me. I'll get impeached."</p><p>Heavy campaigning is not the only strategy Trump has deployed so far. Over the last year, he has also pushed for conservative-leaning states like Texas to redraw their congressional districts to create more opportunities for Republicans to win House seats.</p><p>“It’s an effort to create more Republican seats to offset potential losses in competitive districts elsewhere," Gillespie explained.</p><p>Trump might also seek to influence the handling of the elections themselves, a role the Constitution assigns to state and local authorities.</p><p>Earlier this month, Trump told NBC News he would accept the results of the midterm elections only if he felt they were “honest". He has repeatedly spread false claims of election rigging.</p><p>Experts say such actions and statements point to how critical the upcoming midterms are to Trump's political future.</p><p>“In the end, I tend to think that the 2026 midterm elections come down to whether President Trump will enjoy two more years of congressional cooperation or will endure two long years of constitutional confrontation,” Lucas explained.</p><p>“President Trump knows that, which is why he is placing himself at the centre of the upcoming elections.”</p></div></div></div></div></div></div></main></div></div></div><div class="container container--footer container--white"><div class="container__inner"><footer class="site-footer"><hr gold_standard.gold_text:
With control of the US Congress in the balance, officials say President Donald Trump plans to campaign as if he were 'on the ballot'.
It was a chilly January afternoon in Iowa, and crowds of supporters ringed the stump-speech stage, festooned in red, white and blue.
The scene could have easily been part of United States President Donald Trump's bid for re-election two years ago.
But last month, he hit the campaign trail yet again with a different mission: winning the midterm elections for the Republican Party.
"I'm here because we're starting the campaign to win the midterms," Trump told the crowd. "We've got to win the midterms."
It is not uncommon for sitting presidents to support their party in the midterm races, even though they themselves are not on the ballot. But as the 2026 midterm primaries approach in March, the Trump administration has made it clear one politician should be centre stage: Trump himself.
Sources close to the White House have indicated that the president is pursuing an aggressive campaign strategy, one that could include near-weekly public rallies.
"He's going to campaign like it's 2024 again," Susie Wiles, his chief of staff and former campaign manager, told The Mom View talk show in December.
Already, Trump visited two critical swing states, Pennsylvania and North Carolina, in December, and he has continued to hold public events in the weeks since, touting his economic record in Iowa and Michigan.
But the strategy comes with risks. Midterms often bring stinging losses for the sitting president's party, and Democrats are hoping to leverage Trump's wilting poll numbers to chip away at the Republican majorities in both chambers of Congress.
"The Democratic Party most likely will frame this midterm election as a national referendum on President Trump," said Liberty University law professor Tory L Lucas.
"The more President Trump plays into that narrative and places himself at the centre of the midterms, the more likely the Republican Party will rise or fall based on his personality and popularity."
Trump campaigns in Clive, Iowa, in January 2026
Trump touts his economic agenda on stage at a campaign-style rally in Clive, Iowa, on January 27 [Charlie Neibergall/AP Photo]
The midterm curse
Part of the rationale behind Trump's plans for heavy campaigning stems from fears of the so-called "midterm curse".
That refers to the idea that a sitting president's party is doomed to lose seats in Congress during the midterm elections.
The pattern holds true even with popular presidents, regardless of party. Lucas pointed out that, since World War II, 18 of the 20 midterm elections have caused the sitting president to shed seats in the House of Representatives.
The two exceptions occurred under Democrat Bill Clinton in 1998 and Republican George W Bush in 2002.
"The president’s party tends to lose House seats in 90 percent of midterm elections in the modern era," Lucas told Al Jazeera.
But rather than receding from the spotlight, Lucas indicated that Trump is leaning into his central role in the midterms as a way to buck the trend.
“By nationalising this race, President Trump is trying to defy the structural gravity that has pulled down predecessors from Franklin D Roosevelt to Barack Obama," he said.
Trump and his allies have acknowledged their steep odds, gesturing in their remarks to the midterm curse. The president even suggested the slump could be linked to voters' mental health.
"Sitting presidents don't seem to do well in the midterms. I guess, over a 50-year period, they won twice. So I don't know what that is," Trump told reporters last month on Air Force One.
"That's something down deep. You'd have to ask really a psychiatrist about that."
A supporter takes a photo of Trump on a cellphone
Donald Trump's supporters attend a campaign-style rally in Clive, Iowa, on January 27, ahead of the midterms [Mark Schiefelbein/AP Photo]
Sagging polls
As the primaries draw closer, though, Trump’s ability to rebound will be put to the test.
Part of the hurdle will be to recover from his sagging approval ratings, which reflect public backlash on issues that are key to his platform, such as immigration and the economy.
For instance, a January poll from The Economist and the analytics firm YouGov found that 57 percent of US citizens disapproved of Trump’s job performance. Only 39 percent approved.
A separate Reuters-Ipsos survey from the same month showed similar results, with 59 percent of respondents disapproving of Trump's tenure so far.
Andra Gillespie, a political science professor at Emory University, believes those statistics should worry Republicans.
“The signs don’t look favourable this year for Republicans,” she said. “I won’t predict the number of seats, but Republicans should be bracing based on those numbers that they’re going to sustain some losses.”
Only four seats divide the Republican majority from the Democratic minority in the 435-member House of Representatives.
In the Senate, Republican control is equally slim. Republicans hold 53 seats, compared to the 47 that caucus with the Democrats.
The House, however, is particularly vulnerable: All 435 slots are up for grabs in November's midterms, whereas only a third of the Senate is.
A cellphone records Trump's rally at Clive, Iowa
A woman films President Donald Trump on her phone as he speaks at a rally in Clive, Iowa [Kevin Lamarque/Reuters]
Nationalising the race
The Republican Party has suffered losses since Trump's return to the presidency last year.
In 2025's off-year races, Democrats notched a handful of victories, from Virginia to New Jersey. Wiles, a close adviser to Trump, has blamed the Republican defeats on Trump’s absence from the ballot.
"Typically, in the midterms, it’s not about who’s sitting in the White House. You localise the election, and you keep federal officials out of it," Wiles explained to The Mom View.
"We’re actually going to turn that on its head and put him on the ballot, because so many of those low-propensity voters are Trump voters."
Her strategy is designed to harness the strong sense of loyalty Trump has engendered in the Republican Party.
The YouGov poll found conservative voters overwhelmingly approved of his job, at a rate of 82 percent. A mid-January CBS News survey found an even higher approval rating — 90 percent — among US adults who identify as Republicans.
“Since 2016, our surveys have all been off because we underestimate the Trump vote consistently," said political scientist Lonna Rae Atkeson.
“Trump has definitely drawn more support from irregular voters, people who don't regularly go to the polls, during presidential elections."
But she questioned whether Trump's endorsement would translate into increased support for down-ballot races.
"We haven't seen that carry over well to the midterms,” said Atkeson. “So it may not turn out well for him.”
But putting Trump "on the ballot", as Wiles suggests, also risks shifting the focus of the midterm races away from local issues.
Instead, experts like Gillespie believe that "nationalising" the midterm races could homogenise both down-ballot candidates and their policy platforms, as they seek to reflect national priorities, not local ones.
“One manifestation of polarisation in American politics is that national issues increasingly supplant local ones,” Gillespie said. “As national politics seep into state and local races, it becomes harder for federal candidates to distinguish themselves from Washington.”
Protesters line up outside a Trump rally in Clive, Iowa
Demonstrators protest outside Donald Trump's campaign-style rally in Clive, Iowa, in January [Kevin Lamarque/Reuters]
A 'two-way street'
Already, Trump has announced dozens of endorsements for Republicans campaigning for the Senate, the House and gubernatorial races.
As lines become blurred between local and national politics, Republican hopefuls must determine to what degree they will associate with Trump.
For some candidates in swing districts, proximity to the president could be viewed as a political liability.
“I think it’s important to keep in mind that this is a two-way street,” said Gillespie.
"It is going to be up to the candidates themselves to determine the extent to which they align themselves with President Trump."
Some Republican incumbents, like Representative Mike Lawler of New York, have already put some distance between themselves and the president after Trump posted a racist video on February 5.
Others, like Republican Thomas Massie of Kentucky, have broken from Trump altogether, leading the president to endorse a primary challenger.
Gillespie said that, in general, she is sceptical of the efficacy of Trump's backing.
The majority of his endorsements tend to go to incumbents, who generally have more favourable odds than newcomers.
"What I’ve observed is, sometimes he has a tendency to endorse people who are already on a trajectory of success, and then he takes credit for it," Gillespie said.
Atkeson too questioned whether Trump's sweeping plans for midterm rallies will actually help the candidates running.
"There's not a lot of evidence that his campaigning for people has a big effect. His coattails don’t seem incredibly large," said Atkeson.
Trump makes a campaign stop at an Iowa diner
President Donald Trump makes a campaign stop at the Machine Shed restaurant in Urbandale, Iowa, on January 27 [Kevin Lamarque/Reuters]
Cooperation or confrontation?
But Trump's motives for aggressive campaigning go beyond simply boosting down-ballot Republicans.
This year's midterms hold high stakes for the president. Maintaining control of the House could determine whether Trump's legislative proposals move forward — and whether he will face impeachment during the final two years of his term.
“If voters allow Republicans to keep control of the House, President Trump will have a friendly House,” said Lucas. “But if Democrats take control, increased oversight and impeachment seem like foregone conclusions.”
Trump himself has warned on multiple occasions that impeachment will be a consequence of losing the midterm races.
"You've got to win the midterms," Trump told a conference of Republican leaders last month.
"Because if we don't win the midterms, it's just going to be — I mean, they will find a reason to impeach me. I'll get impeached."
Heavy campaigning is not the only strategy Trump has deployed so far. Over the last year, he has also pushed for conservative-leaning states like Texas to redraw their congressional districts to create more opportunities for Republicans to win House seats.
“It’s an effort to create more Republican seats to offset potential losses in competitive districts elsewhere," Gillespie explained.
Trump might also seek to influence the handling of the elections themselves, a role the Constitution assigns to state and local authorities.
Earlier this month, Trump told NBC News he would accept the results of the midterm elections only if he felt they were “honest". He has repeatedly spread false claims of election rigging.
Experts say such actions and statements point to how critical the upcoming midterms are to Trump's political future.
“In the end, I tend to think that the 2026 midterm elections come down to whether President Trump will enjoy two more years of congressional cooperation or will endure two long years of constitutional confrontation,” Lucas explained.
“President Trump knows that, which is why he is placing himself at the centre of the upcoming elections.”

aria-atomic="true"><p>Hala Salem Darwish, 18, is on life support inside Al-Aqsa Martyrs Hospital in Gaza’s Deir el-Balah. Another Palestinian, 13-year-old Mohammad Saber al-Sheikh, has been battling for life at a hospital in the occupied West Bank.</p><p>Hala was preparing dinner for her family and Mohammad was playing football when they were shot in the head by Israeli snipers.</p><section class="more-on"><h2 class="more-on__heading">Recommended Stories<!-- --> </h2><span class="screen-reader-text">list of 4 items</span><ul class="more-on__list"><li class="more-on__article"><span class="screen-reader-text">list 1 of 4</span><a class="more-on__link" href="/news/2026/4/17/why-are-anti-war-protests-in-the-west-muted-on-iran">Why are anti-war protests in the West muted on Iran?</a></li><li class="more-on__article"><span class="screen-reader-text">list 2 of 4</span><a class="more-on__link" href="/news/2026/4/20/uk-universities-pay-to-spy-on-students-social-media-accounts">British universities paid security firm to ‘spy’ on pro-Palestine students</a></li><li class="more-on__article"><span class="screen-reader-text">list 3 of 4</span><a class="more-on__link" href="/opinions/2026/4/20/the-eu-must-not-wait-till-israel-starts-executing-palestinians">The EU must not wait till Israel starts executing Palestinians</a></li><li class="more-on__article"><span class="screen-reader-text">list 4 of 4</span><a class="more-on__link" href="/news/2026/4/21/spain-slovenia-ireland-push-eu-to-debate-israel-pact-suspension">Spain, Slovenia, Ireland push EU to debate Israel pact suspension</a></li></ul><span class="screen-reader-text">end of list</span></section><p>Their stories, unfolding nearly 100km (62 miles) apart, highlight the persistent and deadly violence faced by Palestinian youth across the occupied Palestinian territory.</p><figure id="attachment_4510134" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-4510134" style="width:770px" class="wp-caption aligncenter"><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" class="size-arc-image-770 wp-image-4510134" src="/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/s33-1776770044.jpg?w=770&resize=770%2C514&quality=80" alt="Hala Salem Darwish, 18, was preparing for her May 1 wedding before an Israeli sniper’s bullet shattered her dreams inside her family home in Maghazi." fetchpriority="low"/><figcaption id="caption-attachment-4510134" class="wp-caption-text">Hala Salem Darwish, 18, was preparing for her May 1 wedding before an Israeli sniper’s bullet shattered her dreams inside her family home in Maghazi [Courtesy of family]</figcaption></figure><h2 id="hala-a-shattered-wedding">Hala: A shattered wedding</h2><p>Hala, who was sheltering in the Maghazi refugee camp in central Gaza, was eagerly looking forward to her wedding, scheduled for May 1. The youngest daughter in her family, she had already prepared her henna and finalised the arrangements for the celebration.</p><p>Those plans were shattered when an Israeli sniper, positioned east of the camp, shot her in the head while she was preparing dinner inside her home near a clinic run by the UN refugee agency for the Palestinians (UNRWA). She may join the more than 72,000 Palestinians killed by Israel since October 2023. Nearly 40,000 of those killed are women and children.</p><p>“She had everything ready for the henna, the wedding, and all the traditions you know,” Mohammad Abu Jabr, a journalist and relative of Hala’s fiance, told Al Jazeera.</p><p>“But the bullet killed this dream.” Hala remains on life support in the intensive care unit at Al-Aqsa Martyrs Hospital in critical condition.</p><figure id="attachment_4510137" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-4510137" style="width:770px" class="wp-caption aligncenter"><img data-recalc-dims="1" loading="lazy" class="size-arc-image-770 wp-image-4510137" src="/wp-content/uploads/2026/04/saass-1776770046.jpg?w=770&resize=770%2C514&quality=80" alt="13-year-old Mohammad Saber al-Sheikh remains in critical condition in Ramallah, with his father, Saber, watching over him after he was shot while playing football." fetchpriority="low"/><figcaption id="caption-attachment-4510137" class="wp-caption-text">13-year-old Mohammad Saber al-Sheikh remains in critical condition in Ramallah, with his father, Saber, watching over him after he was shot while playing football [Al Jazeera/screengrab]</figcaption></figure><h2 id="mohammad-football-under-fire">Mohammad: Football under fire</h2><p>In the occupied West Bank, a similar tragedy struck the Jalazone refugee camp on April 9. Thirteen-year-old Mohammad was playing outside his home when Israeli forces raided the camp amid heavy gunfire.</p><div class="container--ads in-article-ads"><div class="ads"><span class="ads__title"> Advertisement </span></div></div><p>Mohammad was struck in the head by an Israeli sniper’s bullet.</p><p>“The bullet entered and exited his head,” his father, Saber al-Sheikh, told Al Jazeera.</p><p>Mohammad is currently receiving treatment at the Istishari Arab Hospital located in Ramallah, in the occupied West Bank. Dr Abdel Wahab Kharousha said that the impact was so severe that a portion of the boy’s brain protruded from the wound.</p><p>Mohammad remains in the neurology intensive care unit under continuous sedation to control brain pressure. “Mohammad is a hardworking boy,” his father said. “He loves playing football … The doctors did not fall short.”</p><h2 id="a-systemic-toll-on-youth">A systemic toll on youth</h2><p>The shootings of Hala and Mohammad are part of a broader surge in Israeli violence against Palestinian minors.</p><p>On Tuesday morning, 16-year-old Mohammad Majdi al-Jabari was killed in Hebron after being struck by a vehicle in the security convoy of an Israeli minister. The vehicle belonged to a company protecting Settlements Minister Orit Strock, according to reports.</p><p>At least four Palestinians were killed, including two boys, 14 and 16, in attacks by Israeli forces and settlers in the occupied West Bank on Tuesday. In Gaza, at least seven Palestinians, including a child, were killed in a series of attacks despite a “ceasefire” in place since October 2025. Israel has killed nearly 800 Palestinians in breach of the US-brokered “ceasefire”.</p><p>According to the Palestinian government media office, Israeli forces have killed 237 children in the occupied West Bank between October 2023 and mid-April.</p><p>Palestinian journalist Marium Barghouti said Israel has “intensified and increasingly violent” attacks in the occupied West Bank, “with the purpose of dispossessing Palestinians from their homes and lands”.</p><p>Thousands of Palestinians have been displaced in the West Bank as settler violence and Israeli crackdowns have spiked. More than 700,000 settlers live in illegal settlements built on private Palestinian land. Settlements are considered illegal under international laws and are seen as the biggest obstacle in the realisation of Palestinian statehood as part of the so-called two-state solution.</p><p>A group of international humanitarian organisations, in a report published on Monday, said that Israeli soldiers have used sexual abuse to force Palestinians out of their homes in the occupied West Bank.</p><p>“Sexualised violence is used to pressure communities, shape decisions about remaining or leaving their homes and land, and alter patterns of daily life,” the <a href="https://www.nrc.no/resources/reports/sexual-violence-and-forcible-transfer-in-the-west-bank" target="_blank" rel="noopener">report</a> said.</p><div class="container--ads in-article-ads"><div
